metadata.title: Document It Like a Pro: Inventory + Setup Guide
metadata.playlist: Shop for Ingredients — Collect Your Madison Data
metadata.source: INFO 7375 Assignment 3 brief — Shop for Ingredients: Collect Your Madison Data

beats.narration_text: Hola — this is Liam. Episode 6 of 7. Same data, different outcome. Two students submit the same workflow with the same record count. One submits a wall of notes. The other submits a formatted PDF with headings, a data inventory table, and a setup guide a peer could follow. The presentation-excellence points are comparative — the best-presented work in the class scores higher. That is not about production quality; it is about twenty extra minutes of formatting. Today you fill the data inventory template, write the setup guide, and assemble them into one clean PDF. No filler. No real keys. A peer who has never seen your project should be able to replicate it from this document. Let's go.

props.segment: Document It Like a Pro: Inventory + Setup Guide
props.command: Fill the data inventory template. Write a replicable setup guide. No real keys. One clean PDF.
props.runningText: same data, different outcome — presentation is graded and comparative…
output: Data Inventory: per-source table + quality statistics block
Setup Guide: install steps, credential locations, common errors
PDF: headings + consistent formatting + no real API keys

beats.narration_text: The Data Inventory has two sections. The per-source table and the statistics block. Per-source table: one row per source, with these columns — source type, the amount collected, the purpose in one sentence, the collection method, and the quality score expressed as X out of Y records passing the quality checklist for that source. The quality score is not a judgment — it is a count. If the RSS feed gave you 40 items and 37 had all required fields, write 37 out of 40. The statistics block is where you put the numbers from Episode 5: total records, clean records, quality rate as a percentage, number of sources, and the status line 'ready for Assignment 4.' These two sections together account for most of the quality section points. Fill them with the actual numbers from your data file. Do not estimate or round — open the file, count, and copy the real number.

artifactLines: Per-source table: source type · amount collected · purpose (one sentence) · method · quality X/Y.
Quality X/Y = count of records passing all required fields for that source — not a judgment.
Statistics block: total records · clean records · quality rate % · number of sources · 'ready for A4'.
Fill with actual numbers from the saved file — open it, count, copy the real number.
Do not estimate or round. The grader is counting too.
props.sparkLine: Actual numbers. Not estimates. Not rounded.

beats.narration_text: The Setup Guide is the document a peer uses to replicate your workflow from scratch. It has four parts. First: what to install and in what order. Node.js first, then n8n, then any additional dependencies. Do not skip the order — n8n depends on Node and will fail to install if Node is not first. Second: where the access codes go. Name each credential required — YouTube Data API key, NewsAPI key, or whatever you used — and describe where in n8n to enter it. Walk through the Credentials menu step by step. Never paste the real key in the document. Write a placeholder like YOUR_NEWSAPI_KEY_HERE. If you paste a real key into a submitted document, that key is now visible to anyone who opens the file. Rotate the key immediately and write the placeholder. Third: how to load and run the workflow. Import the JSON, run it, confirm output. Fourth: three common errors you encountered and how you fixed them. That last section is what elevates a setup guide from a template-fill to something actually useful.

artifactLines: 1. Install order: Node.js first → n8n → any other deps. Order matters — n8n requires Node.
2. Credential locations: name each key, describe where to enter it in n8n. NEVER paste real keys.
Use placeholder: YOUR_NEWSAPI_KEY_HERE. Real key in a document = rotated key + fix the doc.
3. Load and run: import the workflow JSON → run → confirm output.
4. Three common errors you hit + how you fixed them. This is what makes the guide useful.
props.sparkLine: Install order. Key placeholders. Real errors. Real fixes.

beats.narration_text: Presentation is graded comparatively. This is the twenty extra minutes that scores higher. Three things that matter. First: use headings. A document with clear section headings — Data Inventory, Statistics, Setup Guide, Install Steps, Credentials, Common Errors — is easier to navigate than a wall of text, and the grader reads thirty of these. Make it easy to find each section. Second: consistent formatting. If you use a table for the per-source data, use a table. If you use bullet points, use them throughout. Mixing styles in the same section looks unfinished. Third: visual hierarchy. Bold the section headings. Use a slightly larger font for the title. Add a header with your name, the assignment number, and the date. These are not decorative choices — they are signals that you read and formatted the document intentionally, not just exported raw text from a notes app. Export as PDF before submitting. A PDF renders the same way on every machine the grader uses.

artifactLines: 1. Headings: Data Inventory · Statistics · Setup Guide · Install Steps · Credentials · Common Errors.
2. Consistent formatting: tables or bullets — pick one style per section and keep it.
3. Visual hierarchy: bold headings · title in larger font · header with name + assignment + date.
Export as PDF — renders identically on every machine the grader opens it on.
Why it matters: presentation is comparative. These 20 minutes are the margin between scores.

beats.narration_text: Verdict for Document It Like a Pro. Data Inventory has two sections: the per-source table with type, amount, purpose, method, and quality X out of Y; and the statistics block with total records, clean records, quality rate, number of sources, and status ready for Assignment 4. Setup Guide has four parts: install order, credential locations with no real keys, how to load and run, and three common errors with fixes. Presentation is comparative: clear headings, consistent formatting, visual hierarchy, exported as PDF. The grader reads thirty of these documents — make it easy to find each section. Never paste a real API key in a submitted document. If you did, rotate the key and fix the document before submission.

props.artifactTitle: Document It Like a Pro: Inventory + Setup Guide
artifactLines: Data Inventory: per-source table (type · amount · purpose · method · quality X/Y) + statistics block.
Statistics: total · clean · quality % · sources · 'ready for A4'.
Setup Guide: install order · credential locations (no real keys) · load-and-run · 3 errors + fixes.
Presentation: headings · consistent style · PDF export.
Real key in a submitted doc: rotate it immediately and fix the document.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Paste this prompt into Claude to fill the data inventory and draft the setup guide in one pass. The prompt reads: I have collected data for an n8n workflow for this problem: [A2 problem in one sentence]. Here are my quality numbers: total records pulled [number], clean records after cleanup [number], quality rate [percentage], sources [list your three sources with URLs]. Fill the data inventory template: one row per source with type, amount, purpose, collection method, and quality X out of Y. Then write a setup guide: install steps in order starting with Node.js, where to enter each API credential in n8n without including any real key values — use placeholders, and three common n8n errors a beginner would encounter when building this workflow with their fixes. That prompt produces a first draft you can edit. Before you copy any section into your final PDF, verify that the numbers match your actual file and that no real keys appear anywhere in the text.

props.segment: Document It Like a Pro: Inventory + Setup Guide
props.command: A2 problem: [one sentence]. Quality numbers: total [N] · clean [N] · rate [%] · sources [list]. Fill the data inventory template (type · amount · purpose · method · quality X/Y). Then write a setup guide: install steps from Node.js, credential locations with placeholders only (no real keys), and 3 common n8n errors + fixes.
props.runningText: verify the numbers match your file and no real keys appear before copying to the PDF…

beats.narration_text: Document It Like a Pro: Inventory + Setup Guide. Liam, in for Bear.
